sources.notes: Statement and easy-version sparse matrix constraints.
Official topic confirms the determinant-as-cycle-covers reduction and base-3 path-count construction.
Luogu writeup used to cross-check the DAG path-count interpretation.

annotation.summary: Construct a small sparse {-1,0,1} matrix whose determinant equals a given x.
annotation.constraints: x <= 1e7 and matrix size <= 80 in the easy version, allowing a logarithmic gadget construction.
annotation.core_idea: Interpret the determinant expansion as weighted cycle covers. Convert a bounded-degree DAG with exactly x source-sink paths into a signed matrix so each path contributes one determinant term. The easy bound allows encoding x with a base-3 or incremental path-count gadget.
tricks: Interpret determinant terms as signed cycle covers.
Reduce cycle covers to counting source-sink paths in a DAG.
Encode x with a bounded-degree base-3 path-count gadget.

solution_variants.name: dag-path-count-base3
solution_variants.summary: Build a DAG with exactly x source-sink paths and convert it to a sparse signed matrix whose determinant counts those paths.

solution_variants.name: tridiagonal-euclidean-recursion
solution_variants.summary: Use a tridiagonal determinant recurrence and reverse an Euclidean-style sequence to realize x.

tags.evidence: The central reduction controls the value of a determinant through its expansion.
The output is an explicit sparse matrix built from graph/matrix gadgets.
The determinant construction is reduced to building a DAG with exactly x source-sink paths.
The easy construction encodes x using base-3 digits/path-count gadgets.
An alternate construction uses leading determinants of tridiagonal matrices.
tags.description: Use determinant recurrences or structural properties of tridiagonal matrices, whose nonzero entries lie on the main diagonal and adjacent diagonals.
tags.created_reason: The tridiagonal determinant recurrence is a reusable construction pattern not captured by generic determinant or matrix tags.

tags.evidence: The tridiagonal variant uses coprime adjacent determinant values in an Euclidean-style recurrence.
